document.title: Magrathea ObjectStore — Architecture Documentation

sections.html:
About arc42

arc42, the template for documentation of software and system architecture.

Template Version 1.0.0. Generated from ARC42 template, 2026-06-11

Created, maintained and © by Dr. Peter Hruschka, Dr. Gernot Starke and contributors. See https://arc42.org.

blocks.html: The Magrathea ObjectStore project implements an AWS S3-compatible object storage built with Spring Boot 4 WebFlux and Java 21.
It provides a lightweight, single-node implementation of the AWS S3 REST API for development, testing, and small-scale deployments.

items.text: S3 API compatibility: implement the in-scope AWS S3 REST API operation set (111/111), including Phase F advanced/specialized operations
Reactive by design: full WebFlux non-blocking I/O, DataBuffer streaming for binary content
Domain-Driven Design: pure domain with zero framework dependencies, hexagonal architecture
Testability: 3-layer testing (JUnit domain, Cucumber BDD integration, AWS CLI compatibility)

rows: Clean DDD code, reactive patterns, comprehensive tests
Cucumber BDD scenarios, AWS CLI compatibility verification
ARC42 documentation, ADRs, C4 diagrams, DDD compliance

blocks.html: The project serves as both a production-quality S3-compatible storage and a reference implementation of Java DDD + Spring Boot 4 reactive patterns with hexagonal architecture.

items.text: Java 21: latest LTS, records, sealed interfaces, pattern matching
Spring Boot 4 WebFlux: reactive non-blocking stack
Maven multi-module: flat sibling modules per bounded context
S3-only HTTP API: public HTTP endpoints MUST be AWS S3-compatible; non-S3 internal REST APIs are not allowed
Pluggable S3 adapter: S3 API lives in s3-reactive-api-adapter and is loaded via Spring Boot auto-configuration, not component scanning
S3 API toggle: s3.api.enabled=false disables S3 routes even when the module is present
RouterFunction: NO @Controller, NO @RequestMapping — functional WebFlux style only
XML serialization: Jackson 3 XML annotated record objects (tools.jackson.dataformat.xml.annotation.*) with XmlMapper, NOT StringBuilder
Jackson 3 XML: dependency tools.jackson.dataformat:jackson-dataformat-xml, NOT com.fasterxml.jackson.dataformat:jackson-dataformat-xml
XML codec: custom JacksonXmlEncoder extending AbstractJacksonEncoder<XmlMapper> must be registered via WebFluxConfigurer or HandlerStrategies
object-store-domain: uses ONLY AWS S3 native terminology — no custom abstractions like BinaryContent
object-store-domain: zero dependencies except test dependencies
No shared-domain module exists

storage-engine-domain, storage-engine-application, storage-engine-infrastructure, and object-store-reactive-repository-storage-engine-infrastructure are fully implemented. They form the Storage Engine bounded context for persistent, scalable object storage, selectable via Spring profile (storage-engine) as an alternative to the in-memory backend
object-store-reactive-infrastructure: reactive repository implementations only; no HTTP API routes
InMemoryReactiveS3ObjectRepository: reactive in-memory S3 object metadata + content store via Mono/Flux
ContentStore is removed; object content is saved through S3ObjectRepository.save(S3ObjectWrite)
S3ObjectWrite and S3ObjectContent are domain interfaces without framework types; their application implementations carry Flux<DataBuffer>
byte[] only exists inside repository content storage internals, never in domain signatures
FORBIDDEN: regex XML parsing — java.util.regex.Pattern, String.contains(), and extractXmlValue() for XML are forbidden. All XML parsing must use Jackson 3 codec
JacksonXmlDecoder mandatory: JacksonXmlCodecConfig must register both the encoder (response) and the decoder (request) for XML. Without the decoder, request.bodyToMono(Dto.class) will not work
Command/Query DTO pattern: every PUT/POST receiving XML uses a Jackson XML-annotated Command DTO; every GET producing XML uses a Query DTO. No generic "request" or "response" DTOs
DTOs in separate files: each Command/Query DTO lives in a dedicated file in the dto/command/ or dto/query/ package. No monolithic files (e.g., no S3Request.java)
No custom parsers: no classes like S3XmlParser wrapping XmlMapper. The framework deserializes directly — S3XmlParser and S3XmlResponses removed, ADR 0008 implemented and verified
Application DTO as Command: XML Command DTOs may coincide with application DTOs (e.g., CorsConfigurationCommand) if they have the required Jackson XML annotations
Mapping in handler: conversion from XML Command to domain value object occurs in the handler, not in a separate parser
Domain tests: pure JUnit, NO Spring context
Integration tests: Cucumber BDD with WebTestClient in s3-reactive-api-adapter
AWS CLI compatibility tests: test-aws-cli.sh, also runnable via Maven profile aws-cli-tests
Coverage verification: OpenClover profile coverage

blocks.html: Magrathea ObjectStore provides an S3-compatible REST API. Users interact with it via HTTP to store and retrieve objects in buckets.
The system context shows: - User: end user interacting via HTTPS — S3-compatible API - Magrathea ObjectStore: Spring Boot 4 reactive S3-compatible object storage

rows: S3 REST API requests (XML/JSON) including analytics (?analytics), inventory (?inventory), metrics (?metrics), intelligent-tiering (?intelligent-tiering), ABAC (?abac), object lock (?object-lock), metadata config (?metadata-config), metadata table config (?metadata-table-config), session (?session), directory bucket (?directory-buckets), torrent (?torrent), restore (?restore), select (?select), rename (?rename), legal hold (?legal-hold), retention (?retention) queries

items.text: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters): domain is pure business logic, application and infrastructure are adapters
DDD with strategic design: ObjectStore domain with reserved storage-engine-* modules for future persistence/storage-engine work
Reactive by design: WebFlux RouterFunction, DataBuffer streaming, native Mono/Flux application and repository interfaces

rows: Native Mono/Flux command/query APIs for non-blocking service composition

blocks.html: ADR 0011 records a course correction: each configuration feature (CORS, Lifecycle, Encryption, etc.) is modeled as a dedicated value object on the Bucket aggregate with specific with*() / without*() methods and a specific domain event type per config change, replacing the earlier flat Bucket.Configuration record.

ADR 0010 records a course correction that completed the reactive migration from ADR 0009. All application services now return Mono<T> or Flux<T> natively (no .join()), handlers chain reactive operators directly (no .subscribeOn()), and repository implementations are fully reactive (no Mono.fromCallable stubs). Runtime effects (CORS validation, Website routing, OwnershipControls enforcement, PublicAccessBlock evaluation, Accelerate headers, RequestPayment enforcement) were added.
All handlers (except S3BucketConfigHandler) follow the extract → delegate → response pattern:

items.text: Extract: S3RequestExtractor parses HTTP headers, path variables, and query parameters into typed primitives and DTOs
Delegate: The handler calls a single reactive service method — no validation, no bucket checks, no ETag computation
Response: S3ResponseBuilder or direct XML serialization converts the service result into an HTTP response

blocks.html: All cross-cutting concerns (validation, bucket existence checks, CORS, website routing, ETag) are postponed to the repository layer.

The S3BucketConfigHandler uses a registry/strategy pattern to dispatch configuration operations without copypasta. Each config type has a ConfigHandlerStrategy implementation registered in ConfigHandlerRegistry; adding a new config type requires only one strategy class and its registration.

items.text: Testability: domain tested with pure JUnit (no Spring), integration tested with Cucumber BDD, S3 compatibility verified with AWS CLI
Simplicity: single-node in-memory stores for metadata and content
Traceability: ADRs document every architectural decision, ARC42 provides full architecture documentation

blocks.html: The system is composed of five container runtime modules:

Blackbox Description: Exposes the S3-compatible REST API via RouterFunction WebFlux. Translates HTTP/XML/binary requests into calls to reactive application services. Serializes XML responses with Jackson 3.

Handlers follow a strict minimal pattern: - Extract HTTP headers, path variables, and query parameters into primitives/DTOs via S3RequestExtractor - Delegate to reactive application services (no validation, no bucket checks, no ETag computation) - Response via S3ResponseBuilder or direct XML serialization
All validation, bucket existence checks, ETag computation, CORS validation, and website routing are postponed to the repository layer.

items.text: Provided Interface: S3 REST API (111/111 in-scope operations)
Required Interface: ReactiveBucketService, ReactiveObjectService, ReactiveMultipartUploadService
Technology: Spring Boot 4 WebFlux, Java 21, RouterFunction, Jackson 3 XML

blocks.html: Blackbox Description: Implements reactive object CRUD, multipart upload, content retrieval, and Phase F advanced object use cases using Mono/Flux. Contains reactive application services (ReactiveObjectService, ReactiveMultipartUploadService), domain aggregates (S3Object, MultipartUpload), and repository ports.

items.text: Provided Interface: ReactiveObjectService, ReactiveMultipartUploadService
Required Interface: BucketRepository (read), S3ObjectCommandRepository, S3ObjectQueryRepository, MultipartUploadCommandRepository, MultipartUploadQueryRepository
Technology: Java 21, Spring @Service, Reactor Mono/Flux, domain aggregates

blocks.html: Blackbox Description: Implements reactive bucket lifecycle, metadata (ACL, tagging), configuration (CORS, lifecycle, policy, encryption, logging, website, notification, replication, request payment, ownership controls, public access block, accelerate), advanced configuration (analytics, inventory, metrics, intelligent-tiering), and Phase F bucket metadata/ABAC/object-lock configuration use cases using Mono/Flux. Contains ReactiveBucketService, the Bucket aggregate root (with dedicated configuration value objects and domain events), and repository ports. Configuration lives inside the Bucket aggregate — there is no separate configuration service.

items.text: Provided Interface: ReactiveBucketService
Required Interface: BucketCommandRepository, BucketQueryRepository
Technology: Java 21, Spring @Service, Reactor Mono/Flux, domain aggregates

blocks.html: Blackbox Description: Implements the Command-Query Separation (CQS) pattern at the application layer. Provides separate command and query repository interfaces for each aggregate (Bucket, S3Object, MultipartUpload). Bridges domain repository ports to reactive persistence adapters.

items.text: Provided Interface: BucketCommandRepository, BucketQueryRepository, S3ObjectCommandRepository, S3ObjectQueryRepository, MultipartUploadCommandRepository, MultipartUploadQueryRepository
Required Interface: Reactive persistence implementations
Technology: Java 21, Mono/Flux, CQS pattern

blocks.html: Blackbox Description: Implements reactive in-memory repositories with ConcurrentHashMap using Mono/Flux. Not an external database — resets when the process stops.

items.text: Provided Interface: InMemoryReactiveBucketRepository, InMemoryReactiveS3ObjectRepository, InMemoryReactiveMultipartUploadRepository
Required Interface: CQS repository interfaces
Technology: Java 21, ConcurrentHashMap, Reactor
Tag: Database

rows: Entry point RouterFunction: defines S3 routes and dispatches requests to handlers
Bucket lifecycle: create, delete, head, list, location, versioning, directory-bucket listing

Bucket configuration: CORS, lifecycle, policy, encryption, logging, website, notification, replication, request payment, ownership controls, public access block, accelerate, analytics, inventory, metrics, intelligent-tiering, ABAC, object lock, bucket metadata/table configuration
Object CRUD, copy, multi-delete, rename, torrent, restore, select, Object Lambda response
Object metadata: ACL, tagging, attributes, legal hold, retention, encryption metadata
Multipart upload lifecycle: initiate, upload, complete, abort, list
CreateSession support for Phase F session management
Shared request predicates, parsing, lookup, error helpers
Reactive application service: object CRUD, metadata, listing, content retrieval via Mono/Flux
Reactive application service: multipart upload session and part lifecycle via Mono/Flux
Domain aggregate: object identity, key, ETag, storage class, metadata
Domain aggregate: multipart state, uploaded parts, completion/abort
Repository port: object metadata and binary content persistence
Read-side repository port: bucket existence validation
Application-layer content boundary: Flux<DataBuffer> without leaking into the domain

Reactive application service: bucket lifecycle, versioning, CORS, lifecycle, policy, encryption, logging, website, notification, replication, request payment, ownership controls, public access block, accelerate, analytics, inventory, metrics, intelligent-tiering, ABAC, object lock, and metadata configuration via Mono/Flux

Domain aggregate root: bucket identity, name, region, storage class, versioning, encryption, and dedicated configuration fields per config type (CORS, Lifecycle, Encryption, Logging, Website, Notification, Replication, Policy, PublicAccessBlock, OwnershipControls, RequestPayment, Accelerate, plus multi-instance maps for Analytics, Inventory, Metrics, Intelligent-Tiering). Emits specific ObjectStoreEvent subtypes per config change (e.g., BucketLifecycleConfigurationChanged, BucketNotificationConfigurationChanged). Each config feature has a dedicated with*() / without*() method — no generic withConfiguration() / flat Configuration record.
Repository port: bucket metadata and configuration persistence
Command repository: bucket writes (save, delete) via Mono<Void>
Query repository: bucket reads (find by id, find by name) via Mono<Bucket>
Command repository: object writes (save, delete) via Mono<Void>
Query repository: object reads (find by id, find by bucket, list) via Mono<Flux<S3Object>>
Command repository: multipart upload writes (save, delete, update parts) via Mono<Void>
Query repository: multipart upload reads (find by upload id, list by bucket) via Mono<Flux<MultipartUpload>>
In-memory reactive BucketRepository: ConcurrentHashMap for bucket aggregates and configuration via Mono/Flux
In-memory reactive S3ObjectRepository: ConcurrentHashMap for metadata and raw bytes via Mono/Flux
In-memory reactive MultipartUploadRepository: ConcurrentHashMap for upload sessions via Mono/Flux

blocks.html: The domain aggregates follow a strict state transition pattern:
Bucket is the root aggregate for bucket management. It enforces all state transitions through dedicated with*() methods:

public interface Bucket {
    // Lifecycle
    Bucket withVersioningEnabled();
    Bucket withVersioningSuspended();
    Bucket withDeleted();

    // Configuration (one per config type)
    Bucket withCorsConfiguration(CorsConfiguration cors);
    Bucket withoutCorsConfiguration();
    Bucket withLifecycleConfiguration(BucketLifecycleConfiguration lifecycle);
    Bucket withoutLifecycleConfiguration();
    Bucket withEncryptionConfiguration(BucketEncryptionConfiguration encryption);
    Bucket withoutEncryptionConfiguration();
    Bucket withLoggingConfiguration(BucketLoggingConfiguration logging);
    Bucket withoutLoggingConfiguration();
    Bucket withWebsiteConfiguration(BucketWebsiteConfiguration website);
    Bucket withoutWebsiteConfiguration();
    Bucket withNotificationConfiguration(BucketNotificationConfiguration notification);
    Bucket withoutNotificationConfiguration();
    Bucket withReplicationConfiguration(BucketReplicationConfiguration replication);
    Bucket withoutReplicationConfiguration();
    Bucket withPolicy(BucketPolicy policy);
    Bucket withoutPolicy();
    Bucket withPublicAccessBlockConfiguration(PublicAccessBlockConfiguration publicAccessBlock);
    Bucket withoutPublicAccessBlockConfiguration();
    Bucket withOwnershipControls(BucketOwnershipControls ownershipControls);
    Bucket withoutOwnershipControls();
    Bucket withRequestPaymentConfiguration(BucketRequestPaymentConfiguration requestPayment);
    Bucket withoutRequestPaymentConfiguration();
    Bucket withAccelerateConfiguration(BucketAccelerateConfiguration accelerate);
    Bucket withoutAccelerateConfiguration();

    // Multi-instance config (analytics, inventory, metrics, intelligent-tiering)
    Bucket withAnalyticsConfiguration(String id, BucketAnalyticsConfiguration analytics);
    Bucket withoutAnalyticsConfiguration(String id);
    Bucket withInventoryConfiguration(String id, BucketInventoryConfiguration inventory);
    Bucket withoutInventoryConfiguration(String id);
    Bucket withMetricsConfiguration(String id, BucketMetricsConfiguration metrics);
    Bucket withoutMetricsConfiguration(String id);
    Bucket withIntelligentTieringConfiguration(String id, BucketIntelligentTieringConfiguration tiering);
    Bucket withoutIntelligentTieringConfiguration(String id);

    // Domain event access
    List<ObjectStoreEvent> domainEvents();
    Bucket clearEvents();
}
S3Object enforces state transitions for object lifecycle, metadata, ACL, tagging, legal hold, retention, and encryption:

public interface S3Object {
    S3Object withDeleted();
    S3Object withEtag(String etag);
    S3Object withStorageClass(StorageClass storageClass);
    S3Object withAcl(AccessControlPolicy acl);
    S3Object withTagging(Map<String, String> tagging);
    S3Object withLegalHold(LegalHold legalHold);
    S3Object withoutLegalHold();
    S3Object withRetention(RetentionPeriod retention);
    S3Object withoutRetention();
    S3Object withEncryption(EncryptionConfiguration encryption);
    S3Object withoutEncryption();
    List<ObjectStoreEvent> domainEvents();
    S3Object clearEvents();
}
MultipartUpload enforces multipart upload lifecycle transitions:

public interface MultipartUpload {
    MultipartUpload withPartUploaded(UploadPart part);
    MultipartUpload withCompleted();
    MultipartUpload withAborted();
    List<ObjectStoreEvent> domainEvents();
    MultipartUpload clearEvents();
}
The S3BucketConfigHandler uses a registry/strategy pattern to dispatch configuration operations:

public class S3BucketConfigHandler {
    private final ConfigHandlerRegistry registry;

    Mono<ServerResponse> handleGet(ServerRequest request, ConfigType type) {
        ConfigHandlerStrategy strategy = registry.lookup(type);
        // strategy performs GET using typed methods
        return strategy.handleGet(request);
    }

    Mono<ServerResponse> handlePut(ServerRequest request, ConfigType type) {
        ConfigHandlerStrategy strategy = registry.lookup(type);
        // strategy performs PUT using typed methods
        return strategy.handlePut(request);
    }

    Mono<ServerResponse> handleDelete(ServerRequest request, ConfigType type) {
        ConfigHandlerStrategy strategy = registry.lookup(type);
        // strategy performs DELETE using typed methods
        return strategy.handleDelete(request);
    }
}

rows: Public HTTP API — only AWS S3-compatible endpoints

blocks.html: The runtime view describes the key interaction scenarios of the Magrathea ObjectStore system. These scenarios illustrate how the building blocks collaborate to implement S3-compatible operations.

Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: PUT /{bucket} 2. s3-reactive-api-adapter → reactive-bucket-management: CreateBucket use case 3. reactive-bucket-management → reactive-repository-application: Save Bucket aggregate via command repository 4. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: Persist Bucket aggregate

Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: PUT /{bucket}/{key} 2. s3-reactive-api-adapter → reactive-object-store: PutObject use case (handler delegates directly — no bucket check in handler) 3. reactive-object-store → reactive-repository-application: Save object metadata and bytes via command repository 4. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: Persist object metadata and bytes
Note: Bucket existence validation is postponed to the repository layer. The handler does not verify bucket existence before delegating.

Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: GET /{bucket}/{key} 2. s3-reactive-api-adapter → reactive-object-store: GetObject use case (handler delegates directly — no bucket check in handler) 3. reactive-object-store → reactive-repository-application: Read object metadata and bytes via query repository 4. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: Read object metadata and bytes from persistence
Note: Bucket existence validation is postponed to the repository layer. The handler does not verify bucket existence before delegating.

Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: POST/PUT/GET/DELETE multipart endpoints 2. s3-reactive-api-adapter → reactive-object-store: Multipart upload lifecycle use cases 3. reactive-object-store → reactive-repository-application: Write multipart upload state via command repository 4. reactive-object-store → reactive-repository-application: Read multipart upload state via query repository 5. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: Persist multipart upload state 6. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: Read multipart upload state from persistence

blocks.title: Scenario 5 — Bucket Configuration (CORS, Lifecycle, Policy, Encryption, Logging, Website, Notification, Replication, Request Payment, Ownership Controls, Public Access Block, Accelerate, Analytics, Inventory, Metrics, Intelligent-Tiering, ABAC, Object Lock, Metadata Configuration)

blocks.html: Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: GET/PUT/DELETE /{bucket}?{config} with query parameter identifying the configuration type 2. s3-reactive-api-adapter → reactive-bucket-management: S3BucketConfigHandler dispatches to ReactiveBucketService.{get|put|delete}{Config} based on query parameter 3. reactive-bucket-management → reactive-repository-application: BucketCommandRepository.{save|delete} or BucketQueryRepository.{find} reads/writes configuration via the Bucket aggregate root 4. reactive-repository-application → reactive-infrastructure: InMemoryReactiveBucketRepository persists updated Bucket aggregate with configuration
Configuration types and query parameters:

blocks.title: Scenario 6 — Phase F Advanced Object Operations

blocks.html: Description: Phase F adds advanced/specialized operations from ADR 0012 while reusing existing RouterFunction and handler patterns.

Flow: 1. User → s3-reactive-api-adapter: sends an advanced S3 request such as RenameObject, RestoreObject, SelectObjectContent, WriteGetObjectResponse, legal hold, retention, torrent, or encryption update. 2. S3PathRouter dispatches by HTTP method, path, query parameter, and headers. 3. S3ObjectOperationsHandler handles advanced object behavior such as rename, torrent, restore, select, and Object Lambda response. 4. S3ObjectMetadataHandler handles metadata-oriented Phase F operations such as legal hold, retention, and encryption update. 5. Reactive services/repositories are used where object or bucket state must be read or updated.
Verification: Phase F is Cucumber-tested; mvn test -pl s3-reactive-api-adapter -am -Dsurefire.failIfNoSpecifiedTests=false completed with 216 tests, 0 failures, and 0 errors.
Description: The reactive chain pattern used throughout the application layer. No blocking .join(), no Mono.fromCallable, no .subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic()).

Flow: 1. Handler receives ServerRequest 2. Handler calls reactive service method — returns Mono<T> or Flux<T> 3. Service method composes repository operations using Mono.defer, Mono.just, Mono.justOrEmpty, Flux.defer, Flux.fromIterable 4. Repository operations use native ConcurrentHashMap lookups wrapped in Mono.just/Mono.justOrEmpty 5. Handler chains operators: .flatMap(service::method), .flatMap(service::method2), .map(domain::toDto) 6. Handler returns ServerResponse.ok().body(…​) or ServerResponse.status(code).body(…​)
Example:

// Handler — no blocking, no thread pool offload
public Mono<ServerResponse> handleGet(ServerRequest request) {
    return bucketService.findBucket(bucketName)
        .flatMap(bucket -> {
            CorsConfiguration config = bucket.corsConfiguration();
            return Mono.justOrEmpty(config)
                .map(GetBucketCorsQuery::from);
        })
        .flatMap(query -> ok().body(BodyInserters.fromValue(query)))
        .switchIfEmpty(Mono.defer(() -> errorResponse(404, "NoSuchBucket")));
}
Description: Runtime effects that execute during request processing to enforce S3 semantics.

Trigger: Any request with Origin header. Behavior: S3BucketConfigHandler checks bucket CORS configuration. If the origin is not allowed, returns 403 Forbidden with AccessForbidden error. If CORS preflight (OPTIONS), validates method, headers, and origin.

Trigger: Request to a bucket configured as a website (?website). Behavior: S3BucketOperationsHandler redirects GET /{bucket} to the configured website endpoint if the bucket has website configuration.
Trigger: PutObject, PutObjectAcl requests. Behavior: If bucket has OwnershipControls with ObjectOwnerRequired, the request must include x-amz-object-ownership header.
Trigger: ACL or policy modification requests. Behavior: If PublicAccessBlock is enabled, blocks public ACLs or public bucket policies.
Trigger: Response to GetObject, HeadObject, GetBucketAccelerateConfiguration. Behavior: If bucket has accelerate configuration, response includes x-amz-accelerate-result header.
Trigger: Requests to buckets with RequestPayment configured. Behavior: If bucket has RequesterPays enabled, requests must include x-amz-request-payer header or return 403 AccessDenied.
Description: System behavior for error conditions.

items.text: NoSuchBucket: Handler returns 404 Not Found with S3 XML <Error><Code>NoSuchBucket</Code>…​
NoSuchKey: Handler returns 404 Not Found with S3 XML <Error><Code>NoSuchKey</Code>…​
BucketAlreadyExists: Handler returns 409 Conflict with S3 XML <Error><Code>BucketAlreadyExists</Code>…​
InvalidArgument: Handler returns 400 Bad Request with S3 XML <Error><Code>InvalidArgument</Code>…​

blocks.html: All error responses use S3WebSupport.errorResponse() which creates a Jackson 3 XML annotated S3Error record and serializes it via the registered JacksonXmlEncoder. Reactive error handling uses Mono.error() and Flux.error() with custom exception classes, caught at the handler level and mapped to S3 XML error responses.
Single node, running locally.

rows: In-memory (ConcurrentHashMap), allocated in the same JVM heap as the server

blocks.html: A Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml are present at the project root for containerized execution:
Dockerfile: - Base: eclipse-temurin:21-jdk - Copy bootstrap-application/target/*.jar - Expose port 8080 - Entrypoint: java -jar bootstrap-application-1.0.0-SNAPSHOT.jar
docker-compose.yml: - Single service magrathea-s3 on port 8080:8080 - Environment variables for configuration
The two-container architecture (s3-server + file-store) is intentionally monolithic for single-node operation. In the future:

items.text: file-store can be replaced by R2DBC + PostgreSQL for relational persistence
file-store can become a separate container for horizontal scaling
s3-server can be clustered with session affinity
storage-engine-* placeholder modules for future storage-engine persistence design

blocks.html: The system follows the hexagonal architecture pattern with three layers:

items.text: Domain (core): pure business logic, zero framework dependencies
Application: orchestration, reactive bridge via Mono/Flux
Infrastructure: HTTP adapter (RouterFunction), persistence adapter (in-memory)

blocks.html: Dependencies flow only inward: infrastructure → application → domain.
All HTTP request handling is reactive:

items.text: RouterFunction instead of @Controller
Flux<DataBuffer> for binary upload/download (never byte[] in handlers)
Mono/Flux throughout the reactive application and infrastructure layers
Schedulers.boundedElastic() only for operations that consume the stream in memory

blocks.html: The object-store-domain module is strictly pure:

items.text: NO Spring imports
NO JPA, NO jakarta.validation
NO reactive types (Mono, Flux, DataBuffer)
Repository interfaces with CompletableFuture (standard Java async)
S3ObjectWrite/S3ObjectContent without framework types

blocks.html: Advanced configuration value objects (BucketAnalyticsConfiguration, BucketInventoryConfiguration, BucketMetricsConfiguration, BucketIntelligentTieringConfiguration) follow the same pattern: immutable records, zero framework dependencies, validation with Objects.requireNonNull.
The system exposes ONLY AWS S3-compatible endpoints:

items.text: Same HTTP signature (methods, headers, query parameters)
Same XML format (ListAllMyBucketsResult, ListBucketResult, Error, etc.)
Same error encoding (NoSuchBucket, NoSuchKey, BucketAlreadyExists)
Pluggable via Spring Boot auto-configuration (s3-reactive-api-adapter attaches as an optional module)
Toggle with s3.api.enabled=false

blocks.html: s3-reactive-api-adapter is an auto-configuring module that plugs into the Spring Boot context:

items.text: META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
S3ApiConfig guarded by @ConditionalOnClass({ ReactiveBucketService.class, ReactiveObjectService.class })
s3.api.enabled=true by default
If s3-reactive-api-adapter is not in the classpath, S3 routes are not loaded

blocks.title: Command/Query DTO Pattern for XML Serialization

blocks.html: The system exposes an XML S3-compatible API where each operation has a structured XML body. Instead of using custom parsers (regex, explicit XmlMapper) or generic "request"/"response" DTOs, the system follows the DDD CQS (Command-Query Separation) pattern:

items.text: Commands: represent the intention to change state (PUT, POST, DELETE). They are Jackson XML-annotated DTOs that the framework deserializes directly from the HTTP body. Examples: CorsConfigurationCommand, LifecycleConfigurationCommand, DeleteObjectsCommand

Queries: represent the response to a read request (GET). They are Jackson XML-annotated DTOs that the framework serializes directly into the HTTP body. Examples: ListBucketsQuery, GetBucketCorsQuery, ErrorResponse

blocks.html:
Benefits:
- Zero custom parsing: Spring Boot 4 WebFlux deserializes/serializes with registered codecs
- Separate DTOs per operation: each Command/Query lives in its own file, organized by domain
- DDD alignment: Commands express intent, Queries express reads — semantically clear
- Maintainability: modifying an XML tag means modifying a single DTO, not a regex parser and a separate mapping
Spring Boot 4 WebFlux handles XML serialization via codecs registered in JacksonXmlCodecConfig:

items.text: JacksonXmlEncoder: serializes response DTOs into XML
JacksonXmlDecoder: deserializes request body into Command DTOs (added with ADR 0008)
Both use tools.jackson.dataformat:jackson-dataformat-xml (Jackson 3, not Jackson 2)
DTOs use tools.jackson.dataformat.xml.annotation.* annotations (@JacksonXmlRootElement, @JacksonXmlProperty, @JacksonXmlElementWrapper, @JacksonXmlText)

blocks.html: The decoder allows request.bodyToMono(CorsConfigurationCommand.class) to work for XML — the framework reads the body, passes it to the XML decoder, and produces the annotated record. No explicit call to XmlMapper.
The application layer separates write and read concerns at the repository level:

items.text: Command repositories: BucketCommandRepository, S3ObjectCommandRepository, MultipartUploadCommandRepository — accept Mono<Void> return types for write operations
Query repositories: BucketQueryRepository, S3ObjectQueryRepository, MultipartUploadQueryRepository — return Mono<T> or Flux<T> for read operations

blocks.html: This enables independent optimization of read and write paths, and aligns with CQS principles at the persistence boundary.
All handlers (except S3BucketConfigHandler) follow the extract → delegate → response pattern:

public Mono<ServerResponse> putObject(ServerRequest request) {
    return objectService.saveObjectWithContent(
            S3RequestExtractor.extractObjectKey(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractStorageClass(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractChecksum(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractEncryption(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractContentType(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractContentLength(request),
            S3RequestExtractor.extractUserMetadata(request),
            request.bodyToFlux(DataBuffer.class))
        .flatMap(result -> S3ResponseBuilder.ok(result.aggregate()))
        .onErrorResume(BucketNotFoundException.class,
            e -> S3WebSupport.xmlError(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND, "NoSuchBucket", "Bucket not found"))
        .onErrorResume(Throwable.class,
            e -> S3WebSupport.xmlError(HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, "InternalError", e.getMessage()));
}

Key constraints: - NO validation — postponed to repository preconditions - NO bucket existence checks — postponed to repository preconditions - NO ETag computation — postponed to repository on store - NO handler-local state — postponed to repository - NO CORS/website logic — postponed to repository/service
The following cross-cutting concerns are intentionally absent from handlers and will be implemented in the repository layer:
Error handling follows the reactive pattern with typed exceptions and S3 XML error serialization:

S3Exception (abstract)
├── NoSuchBucketException     → 404 NoSuchBucket
├── NoSuchKeyException        → 404 NoSuchKey
├── BucketAlreadyExistsException → 409 BucketAlreadyExists
├── InvalidArgumentException  → 400 InvalidArgument
├── AccessDeniedException     → 403 AccessDenied
└── InternalServerException   → 500 InternalServerError

// Handler-level error handling
public Mono<ServerResponse> handleGet(ServerRequest request) {
    return service.findBucket(name)
        .flatMap(bucket -> service.getObject(bucket, key))
        .map(object -> buildResponse(object))
        .onErrorResume(NoSuchBucketException.class, e -> errorResponse(404, "NoSuchBucket"))
        .onErrorResume(NoSuchKeyException.class, e -> errorResponse(404, "NoSuchKey"))
        .onErrorResume(InvalidArgumentException.class, e -> errorResponse(400, "InvalidArgument"))
        .onErrorResume(throwable -> errorResponse(500, "InternalError"));
}
All error responses use S3WebSupport.errorResponse() which creates a Jackson 3 XML annotated S3Error record.
All three aggregates (Bucket, S3Object, MultipartUpload) follow the same pattern:

items.text: State transitions return NEW immutable instances — no mutation
Each transition records a domain event in an accumulated list
domainEvents() retrieves pending events
clearEvents() returns a clean instance ready for persistence
State transitions return NEW immutable instances with accumulated events
domainEvents() retrieves pending events; clearEvents() returns a clean instance for persistence

blocks.html: Each event carries the specific value object type (e.g., BucketLifecycleConfiguration, BucketNotificationConfiguration) so consumers can react to precise changes.

The system must handle headers that the AWS CLI v2 sends by default on every aws s3api put-object request. These headers are critical for AWS CLI compatibility but are currently ignored by the handler.

blocks.title: Default Headers Sent by AWS CLI v2

rows: Payload integrity hash computed automatically by AWS CLI

blocks.html: When --checksum-algorithm <alg> is specified, AWS CLI v2 automatically computes the hash and sends it in the corresponding header:

The server must read x-amz-sdk-checksum-algorithm, re-compute the hash of the received body with that algorithm, and compare against the corresponding x-amz-checksum-<alg> header. A mismatch must return 400 InvalidArgument (ChecksumMismatch).

AWS CLI v2 does NOT send Content-Type by default unless --content-type is explicitly passed. The handler currently falls back to application/octet-stream. When AWS CLI is used, the stored content-type is incorrect — the object will be served with application/octet-stream instead of the actual content type.

rows: No data integrity validation — corrupted payloads accepted silently
No authentication — anyone can write/read objects
Content-Type not sent by AWS CLI
Wrong content-type stored when using AWS CLI

blocks.html: Three levels of testing:

items.text: Domain: Pure JUnit, zero Spring — test invariants and business rules (213 tests)
Application: Reactive application service tests (18 tests)
S3 Integration: Cucumber BDD with WebTestClient — verify HTTP endpoints (227 tests)
AWS CLI Compatibility: test-aws-cli.sh bash script + mvn verify -Paws-cli-tests

blocks.html: Total: 479 tests (213 domain + 18 application + 227 S3 integration + 21 AWS CLI), 12 failures (12 anomaly tests documenting known bugs in put_object handler — see PLAN.md Phase 0 for details).
All architectural decisions are documented as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) in docs/adr/.

rows: Single-node in-memory only — reactive in-memory repositories do not persist to disk
Introduce file persistence or R2DBC; storage-engine-* placeholder modules are reserved for this work
S3 semantic depth — 111/111 operations are routed and Cucumber-tested, but some advanced operations use simplified compatibility behavior
AWS SDK features may expect deeper semantics than the in-memory implementation provides
Track semantic gaps in PLAN.md and ADR follow-ups; prioritize AWS CLI/SDK compatibility cases
In-memory content store — large objects stored in ConcurrentHashMap
Flush to file system, chunked streaming, content store with file persistence
No storage-engine integration — placeholder modules are empty
Not a v1 priority — planned as future storage-engine architecture work

Missing checksum validation — x-amz-checksum-crc64nvme and x-amz-sdk-checksum-algorithm headers are not read by the PutObject handler. AWS CLI v2 sends these by default and computes the hash automatically. The server should re-compute and verify.
Corrupted payloads accepted silently — data integrity not verified
Read x-amz-sdk-checksum-algorithm, re-compute hash with that algorithm, compare with x-amz-checksum-crc64nvme; return 400 InvalidArgument on mismatch
